context: USS Abraham Lincoln Carrier Strike Group deployed to the Arabian Gulf in January 2026 as part of the U.S. Central Command's response to Iran's refusal to resume nuclear negotiations and its declared intent to increase uranium enrichment to weapons-grade levels. The strike group's positioning near the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 20% of global oil supply transits — was explicitly tied to deterring any Iranian attempt to close the waterway in retaliation for expanded U.S. and Israeli economic pressure. CENTCOM confirmed the deployment publicly, a deliberate departure from routine ambiguity designed to signal resolve ahead of a U.S.-imposed diplomatic deadline.

USS Harry S. Truman Carrier Strike Group deployed for a second rotation to the Red Sea in November 2025 following a brief turnover period, resuming flight operations against remaining Houthi surface-to-air missile and drone infrastructure that had survived earlier Operation Rough Rider strikes. Despite significant degradation of Houthi offensive capacity during the March-July 2025 campaign, Iran-supplied anti-ship ballistic missiles and one-way attack drones continued to threaten commercial shipping transiting the Bab-el-Mandeb strait. The Truman CSG's second deployment reflected CENTCOM's assessment that Houthi residual capability required continued suppression to restore full commercial shipping confidence.

to_location: Diego Garcia, British Indian Ocean Territory
context: A second B-52H task force from the 2nd Bomb Wing rotated into Diego Garcia in September 2025, sustaining the bomber presence that began with Operation Rough Rider and transitioning the mission from active strike operations against Houthis to a broader strategic deterrence posture aimed at Iran. The rotation included upgraded aircraft carrying the Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile-Extended Range (JASSM-ER), providing a long-range conventional strike option against hardened Iranian facilities without requiring overflight of contested airspace. Defense officials confirmed the bombers had conducted dual-key rehearsals with B61-12 gravity bomb delivery profiles, a deliberate nuclear signaling element intended to underline the credibility of the U.S. extended deterrence commitment to Gulf partners.

to_location: Diego Garcia, British Indian Ocean Territory
context: B-2 Spirit stealth bombers from the 509th Bomb Wing deployed to Diego Garcia in February 2026 as Iran-U.S. tensions over the nuclear program reached their most acute point since 2020, coinciding with the expiration of a U.S.-imposed 90-day diplomatic ultimatum. The B-2s, capable of penetrating Iranian integrated air defenses that would defeat conventional strike packages, represent the credible hard-power backstop to diplomatic negotiations. The deployment was confirmed by satellite imagery analysis published by commercial providers before the Pentagon formally acknowledged the forward presence — a deliberate sequencing that allowed the U.S. to shape Iranian threat perceptions without triggering a formal crisis escalation.

asset_name: Battery A, 11th Air Defense Artillery Brigade (THAAD)
asset_type: Terminal High Altitude Area Defense Battery
context: A THAAD battery from the 11th Air Defense Artillery Brigade deployed to Al Udeid Air Base in late January 2026, reinforcing the base's existing Patriot coverage amid intelligence assessments that Iran had forward-deployed ballistic missiles to sites capable of striking U.S. facilities across the Gulf Cooperation Council area within minutes of launch. Al Udeid hosts the USAF's largest overseas air base and the Combined Air Operations Center (CAOC) that controls all air operations across the CENTCOM theater, making it the highest-priority defensive target in any Iran contingency. The THAAD system's 200-kilometer intercept range provides a layered defense architecture alongside Patriot PAC-3 batteries already in country.

USS Cole and two additional Arleigh Burke-class destroyers conducted a high-visibility freedom of navigation transit through the Strait of Hormuz in early February 2026, the first such publicly announced U.S. naval passage since Iran formally warned it would treat any U.S. warship transit as a provocation. The transit was deliberately conducted on the surface, in daylight, and with full AIS broadcasting — a maximal transparency posture intended to expose the hollowness of Iran's threat rather than provoke a miscalculation. Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy patrol boats shadowed the formation at safe distances; no hostile actions were taken.

An F-15EX Eagle II detachment from the 366th Fighter Wing at Mountain Home AFB rotated into Al Dhafra Air Base in August 2025, replacing F-15E Strike Eagles that had been providing air superiority cover over the Arabian Gulf throughout Operation Rough Rider. The F-15EX's advanced Eagle Passive/Active Warning Survivability System (EPAWSS) and capacity to carry 22 air-to-air missiles make it the Air Force's most capable beyond-visual-range fighter in contested Gulf airspace. The rotation coincided with intelligence reporting that Iran had activated long-dormant air defense radar networks along its southern coastline, suggesting elevated Iranian alertness to U.S. air operations.

asset_name: Joint Special Operations Task Force – Horn of Africa
context: AFRICOM's Joint Special Operations Task Force – Horn of Africa conducted a rotational troop uplift in October 2025 at Camp Baledogle in central Somalia, increasing the advisory and direct action capacity available to counter al-Shabaab resurgence following a series of coordinated attacks that overran three Somali National Army positions in the Jubba Valley. U.S. Army Special Forces ODAs expanded training pipelines for Danab Brigade — Somalia's elite counterterrorism force — while AFSOC AC-130J Ghostrider gunships provided persistent overwatch for Somali-led clearance operations in the Lower Jubba region. The uplift brought estimated U.S. force levels in Somalia back toward the ~700-troop ceiling that had characterized the pre-2021 drawdown posture.

asset_name: MQ-9 Reaper Task Force, 60th Expeditionary Attack Squadron
context: The 60th Expeditionary Attack Squadron expanded its MQ-9 Reaper operations at Camp Lemonnier in January 2026, increasing the number of continuously orbiting ISR tracks over Somalia, the Red Sea, and the western Indian Ocean in response to simultaneous operational demands from AFRICOM and CENTCOM. Camp Lemonnier, the only permanent U.S. military installation on the African continent, serves as the primary logistics and launch hub for drone operations covering the entire Horn of Africa and supports maritime patrol missions coordinating with the Combined Maritime Forces in the Gulf of Aden. The expanded MQ-9 footprint reflected a dual-theater pressure dynamic: Houthi maritime threats required persistent Red Sea coverage while al-Shabaab operational tempo demanded unbroken Somalia surveillance.

USS Carl Vinson Carrier Strike Group entered the South China Sea in early January 2026 for the first carrier presence operation of the new year, conducting dual-carrier exercises with USS George Washington in the Philippine Sea before Vinson proceeded south through the contested waters. The deployment followed a December 2025 incident in which a PLA Navy destroyer conducted an unsafe intercept of a Philippine Coast Guard vessel near Scarborough Shoal, prompting INDOPACOM to accelerate Vinson's western Pacific deployment timeline. The strike group's air wing conducted simulated attacks on surface combatants during the transit, with tracks analyzed by satellite imagery as targeting ingress profiles consistent with the Chinese Type-055 cruiser class.

Exercise Balikatan 2026, scheduled for April-May 2026, is expected to build on the 2025 iteration's record scale with an expanded Typhon Mid-Range Capability (MRC) live-fire component and the first deployment of the Navy-Marine Expeditionary Ship Interdiction System (NMESIS) to Philippine territory, providing a ground-launched anti-ship missile battery operated jointly with Philippine Marine Corps crews. The exercise is also expected to formalize the activation of two additional EDCA sites in Cagayan province, positioning U.S. access infrastructure within 350 kilometers of the Taiwan Strait's southern approaches. Philippine and U.S. planners have publicly described Balikatan 2026 as a rehearsal for "integrated deterrence" rather than a routine bilateral exercise.

primary_source: I Marine Expeditionary Force Public Affairs
context: Marine Rotational Force – Darwin 26.1 is slated to arrive at Robertson Barracks in April 2026 as the most capable iteration of the rotation to date, incorporating an increased F-35B complement drawn from two VMFA squadrons and a Marine Littoral Regiment (MLR) element intended to rehearse dispersed island-hopping operations across the eastern Indian Ocean and Timor Sea. The MLR integration represents a doctrinal shift from the MRF-D's original focus on amphibious assault toward the stand-in force concept described in Marine Corps Force Design 2030, emphasizing anti-ship fires and logistics resilience over massed amphibious landings. Australia has agreed to co-fund permanent infrastructure improvements at RAAF Darwin and RAAF Tindal to support the expanded aviation footprint.

asset_name: B-52H Stratofortress, 5th Bomb Wing (Det.) – Continuous Bomber Presence
context: The 5th Bomb Wing rotated a B-52H task force into Andersen Air Force Base in early February 2026 as part of the Air Force's uninterrupted Continuous Bomber Presence mission that has maintained a strategic bomber footprint in the Pacific since 2004. The rotation included integration sorties with Japan Air Self-Defense Force F-2s, Royal Australian Air Force F/A-18F Super Hornets, and Republic of Korea Air Force F-35As, validating combined strike packages against simulated naval task force targets. PACAF has incrementally expanded CBP mission scope to include anti-surface warfare profiles, reflecting a shift from pure land-attack deterrence toward supporting the joint force's maritime campaign priorities against PRC naval formations.

asset_name: 388th Fighter Wing F-35A (Det.), Kadena Air Base
context: A detachment of F-35A Lightning IIs from Hill AFB's 388th Fighter Wing rotated into Kadena Air Base in February 2026, continuing the rolling rotational presence that replaced Kadena's permanently-stationed F-15C/D Eagle fleet. The rotation focused on advanced low-observable penetration profiles into simulated contested environments over the East China Sea, with particular emphasis on time-sensitive targeting procedures against mobile launchers — a capability gap identified during previous rotations. U.S. and Japanese officials have indicated a decision on a permanent fifth-generation basing arrangement at Kadena is expected before the end of 2026, with F-35A and F-15EX hybrid wing configurations under active consideration.

Exercise Cobra Gold 2026 opened in mid-February with approximately 10,000 U.S. personnel and forces from eight partner nations, placing particular emphasis on joint logistics over-the-shore (JLOTS) operations and medical interoperability — capabilities directly relevant to a Taiwan or South China Sea contingency requiring sustained combat resupply across open ocean. Thailand's continued hosting of the exercise despite domestic political pressure to reduce security dependence on Washington underscores the alliance's resilience and Bangkok's long-term strategic calculus favoring U.S. engagement. The 2026 exercise introduced a new cyber defense component in response to the growing targeting of INDOPACOM exercise networks by state-sponsored intrusion operations.

asset_type: Amphibious Ready Group / Marine Expeditionary Unit
primary_source: I Marine Expeditionary Force Public Affairs
context: The 15th Marine Expeditionary Unit embarked aboard the Boxer Amphibious Ready Group departed San Diego in early March 2026 for the Western Pacific, providing 7th Fleet with a forward-deployed crisis response force capable of scalable operations from noncombatant evacuation to forcible entry across the Philippine Sea and South China Sea. The MEU's BLT 1/5 infantry battalion completed specialized training in urban and mountainous terrain relevant to Taiwan strait island seizure scenarios before deployment. The Boxer ARG-15th MEU is expected to participate in Exercise Balikatan 2026 in the Philippines and conduct presence operations in the South China Sea before a planned port call in Vietnam.

to_location: Orzysz / Bemowo Piskie Training Area, Poland
primary_source: U.S. Army Europe and Africa Public Affairs
context: The 2nd Cavalry Regiment forward-deployed a reinforced Stryker squadron to northeastern Poland in January 2026 under the Atlantic Resolve mission, maintaining the continuous armored presence in the Suwalki Gap region that has persisted since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. The regiment's positioning at Bemowo Piskie, co-located with Polish Army's 15th Mechanized Brigade and a permanent rotational U.S. battalion, creates a combined arms force capable of immediate defensive operations along the most vulnerable terrain feature on NATO's eastern flank. January's rotation incorporated newly fielded Stryker Short Range Air Defense (SHORAD) vehicles — a direct response to the proliferation of drone warfare in the Ukraine conflict and identified vulnerabilities in armored formations operating without organic air defense.

primary_source: U.S. Army Europe and Africa Public Affairs
context: The 101st Airborne Division's 2nd Brigade Combat Team rotated into Romania in November 2025, sustaining the air assault presence that the Screaming Eagles have maintained on NATO's southeastern flank since 2022. The brigade's co-location with Romanian and Bulgarian forces at Kogalniceanu provides NATO's Black Sea flank with a rapid-reaction air assault capability that can respond to contingencies in the Western Balkans, Moldova, or across the Black Sea to Georgia within 24 hours. The rotation added a Remotely Piloted Aircraft platoon operating Gray Eagle UAS for persistent surveillance of the Black Sea littoral, filling a reconnaissance gap identified after Turkey restricted ISR overflight to manage its own equities in the Russia-Ukraine conflict.

USS Gerald R. Ford Carrier Strike Group deployed to the Eastern Mediterranean and conducted a high-profile port call at Souda Bay, Crete before proceeding to the North Atlantic for Exercise Steadfast Defender exercises with NATO allies in November 2025. The Ford's Eastern Mediterranean passage followed a period of increased Russian naval activity including a surge of Russian submarines departing Murmansk for Atlantic patrol, prompting NATO Supreme Allied Commander Europe to request a visible allied carrier presence in the theater. The strike group's integration with NATO's Standing Naval Force Atlantic demonstrated the U.S. Navy's capability to contribute to both CENTCOM and EUCOM requirements on a single deployment arc.

to_location: Ādaži Military Base, Latvia / Tapa Army Base, Estonia
primary_source: U.S. Army Europe and Africa Public Affairs
context: The 173rd Airborne Brigade Combat Team conducted a rapid deployment exercise to Latvia and Estonia in late February 2026 — timed to coincide with the fourth anniversary of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine — rehearsing the forced-entry and defensive reinforcement tasks the brigade would execute in the opening hours of any Russian incursion against a Baltic NATO ally. The operation validated the Airborne Task Force concept under which the 173rd can deploy a ready battalion to any NATO airfield within 18 hours of alert, providing an immediate visible commitment before heavier follow-on forces can arrive. Estonian and Latvian political leaders attended the culminating airborne assault demonstration alongside SACEUR, signaling the exercise's dual military-political deterrence function.
